title: Fabada asturiana
subtitle: Asturian bean and pork stew built around fat white fabes and smoked sausage.
excerpt: The big bean stew of northern Spain. White Asturian beans cooked slowly with chorizo, morcilla, and pancetta until the broth thickens into a rich coral-coloured sauce. Two hours mostly hands-off; serves six generously; the leftovers are arguably better.
sourceNotes:
Asturian regional cookery, particularly the Cangas del Narcea and central inland villages where fabes de la Granja are grown. Documented in Spanish regional cookbooks across the twentieth century and codified by the Denominación de Origen Faba Asturiana, which protects the bean variety since the 1990s.

The canonical version uses three pork products together: chorizo, morcilla, and pancetta (or salt-cured pork shoulder). The smoke and the cured fat carry the broth.

Fat butter beans stand in well for fabes if you cannot source the proper Asturian beans. Soak overnight either way; old beans turn the stew gritty.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes excellently for up to three months. Cool fully, portion into freezer-friendly containers leaving room for expansion, and defrost overnight in the fridge. Reheat gently on the hob with a splash of water if the broth has thickened too far.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles for a crowd. Use a wide deep pan so the beans sit no more than 8 cm deep and cook evenly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fabada is at its best on day two. Make it the day before; the broth thickens overnight and the smoke from the chorizo settles through the beans.

recipeTools.notes: A heavy 5-litre stockpot or Dutch oven. The beans need depth of broth to cook evenly; a wide shallow pan reduces too fast.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fat white Asturian bean variety, with skin that stays intact during long cooking. Protected by a Spanish Denominación de Origen since 1990. Substitute butter beans or large cannellini if you cannot source them.

content.text: Fabada is the big winter bean stew of Asturias, the green northern province between the mountains and the Cantabrian Sea. Fat white beans, smoked sausage, blood pudding, and cured pork, simmered slowly until the broth turns coral pink with chorizo paprika and the beans give up their starch. Two hours of mostly hands-off cooking and a pot that feeds six for two meals.
The classical bean is the
fabes de la Granja
, a fat white Asturian bean with a skin that holds intact through the long cook. Spanish shops carry them dried. Outside Spain, fat butter beans work well; large cannellini stand in at a push.

Three pork products go in together. Chorizo for smoke and paprika. Morcilla for sweetness and the dark colour that streaks through the broth. Pancetta or a piece of salt-cured pork shoulder for the cured fat that carries the flavour. The three together are the canonical compango; leaving one out changes the dish.
What you need

items.prepNote: dried, soaked overnight in plenty of cold water
cooking chorizo, semi-cured, in two whole sausages
in a single piece, rind on if possible
in one piece, salted, optional second piece of cured pork
a generous pinch, bloomed in 2 tbsp warm water
to taste, only at the end

content.text: You also need a heavy 5-litre stockpot or a Dutch oven with a lid. The beans need depth of broth to cook evenly; a wide shallow pan reduces too fast and the top beans dry out.
Method
Soak the beans overnight
Cover {{butter-beans}} of dried beans with plenty of cold water. Leave overnight, at least twelve hours. The beans triple in volume; give them room. Drain and rinse before cooking.
Build the pot

Tip the drained beans into the stockpot. Add {{chorizo}} of chorizo, {{pancetta}} of pancetta, and {{pork-shoulder}} of salted pork shoulder if using. Tuck the {{onion}} halved onion and {{garlic}} of garlic between the beans. Add {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Pour over enough cold water to cover the beans by 5 cm. Add the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil.

Bring slowly to a bare simmer, skimming any grey foam that rises in the first ten minutes. Once the broth is clear, lower the heat so only an occasional bubble breaks the surface. Cook with the lid ajar for an hour and a half.
Asustar the beans

After thirty minutes and again at the hour mark, pour in a small cup of cold water. The shock stops the beans climbing to a boil and helps the skins stay intact. This trick is the only piece of technique that fabada really turns on.
Add saffron and paprika

At the hour-and-a-half mark, taste a bean. It should be tender to the bite but holding shape. Stir in {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika and the saffron with its soaking water. The broth turns from cream to coral. Cook on for another thirty to forty-five minutes. The bean skins will be soft and the broth will have thickened to the consistency of single cream.
Rest, then serve

Pull the pot off the heat and leave to settle for thirty minutes off the heat. The broth thickens and the flavours deepen. Salt to taste only now; the cured pork is salty and an early addition will go too far. Lift the meat onto a board, slice the chorizo, pancetta, and pork into thick rounds. Ladle the beans into shallow bowls, lay slices of each meat over the top, and serve with country bread.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Fabada is forgiving once the beans are in. The two failure modes are old beans and a hard boil.

items.fix: Dried beans lose moisture and turn gritty after a year. Buy fresh stock with a clear pack date. A full twelve hours of overnight soaking is the minimum; longer does no harm.

items.symptom: Bean skins split and the broth is cloudy.
items.fix: Fabada wants the gentlest simmer in the trade. The asustar trick (cold water shocks at thirty and sixty minutes) is the easiest correction; keeping the heat very low does the rest.

items.symptom: Broth too thin at the end.
items.cause: Beans had too much water, or pot too wide.
items.fix: Lift out the meat and a ladleful of beans. Set the pot back on the heat with the lid off and simmer until the broth thickens to single-cream consistency, then return everything to the pot.
Most cured pork is heavily salted. Add salt only at the end, by taste. If the broth is over-salty already, drop a peeled raw potato in for ten minutes and lift it out; it pulls some salt.

content.text: Variations

Fabada is hard to vegetarianise without becoming a different dish; the smoke and cured fat are central. A vegetarian Asturian bean stew works well as a separate recipe, built around smoked paprika and roasted onions.
Some cooks add half a smoked ham hock when they can find one, which goes in with the beans and adds another layer of cured-pork flavour. A bone from a leftover Sunday roast slips into the pot too.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers
Fabada is one of those stews that improves on day two. Cool fully, refrigerate, and reheat gently with a splash of water if the broth has gelled. Three days in the fridge is the safe window.

Freezes well for up to three months in portioned containers. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat slowly on the hob. The bean skins can split slightly on freezing; cook only just enough on day one so they hold up.
Where this dish lives

Fabada is the regional emblem of Asturias and one of the better-known Spanish bean stews internationally. The dish belongs to the cooler, wetter northern half of the peninsula, where bean stews and cider replace gazpacho and sherry. Sunday lunch territory; festival territory; the bowl you serve when the weather turns and the woodstove is on.

The bean variety has been documented since at least the seventeenth century. The full pork compango became standard later, as smallholder pig-rearing in Asturias produced the curing tradition that fabada now relies on. A bowl of fabada and a glass of cold cider remains the canonical pairing.
